benchmarks.name: Crash Recovery Testing
benchmarks.value: 10,000 sequences, 0 failures

benchmarks.name: WAL Recovery Testing
benchmarks.value: 1,000 sequences, 0 failures

benchmarks.name: Durability Model
benchmarks.value: CoW WAL (not ARIES)

benchmarks.name: WAL Integrity
benchmarks.value: HMAC-SHA384 chained format (PR #247) — each tag covers prev_tag + WalProvenance + data. 33 WAL tests total (9 HMAC, 14 WAL, 8 epoch WAL, 2 partitioned WAL)
benchmarks.source: PR #247. Chained HMAC format: [0xCB magic][provenance:26B][record:N][hmac_tag:48B]. WalProvenance per record: agent_id (16B), clearance (1B), connectivity (1B), write_ts_ms (8B). HMAC chain: each tag = HMAC(key, prev_tag || provenance || data) — tampering any record invalidates all subsequent. RDKC-derived or static key modes. ACVP test vectors (RFC 4231). CNSA 2.0 compliant.

sections.narrative: TrustDB's durability model is strong but young compared to battle-tested systems like SQLite (decades of production) and RocksDB (Meta-scale). The 10K DST crash sequences with zero data loss is encouraging, but SQLite has survived billions of production deployments. TrustDB's CoW WAL design is architecturally similar to LMDB (both use copy-on-write), which is known for excellent crash safety. PR #247 upgraded WAL integrity from FNV-1a (non-cryptographic) to HMAC-SHA384 with chained format — each record's tag covers the previous tag plus WalProvenance (agent_id, clearance, connectivity, timestamp), making tampering cryptographically detectable. 33 WAL tests total across 4 modules. The key differentiator: TrustDB maintains durability guarantees while also propagating confidence metadata and provenance through the WAL with cryptographic non-repudiation (NIST AU-10) — something no competitor does.

benchmarks.name: Isolation Level
benchmarks.value: Serializable (SSI)

benchmarks.name: Structural Invariants Enforced
benchmarks.value: 5 invariants

benchmarks.name: Confidence Propagation Invariant
benchmarks.value: Enforced at flush

sections.narrative: TrustDB provides Serializable Snapshot Isolation — the strongest isolation level. This matches SQLite and is stronger than Neo4j (Read Committed) and default RocksDB (Snapshot Isolation). PostgreSQL offers Serializable but it's optional and has significant performance overhead. TrustDB's unique advantage: 5 structural invariants including confidence propagation and provenance chain integrity, which no competitor offers because they don't have epistemic concepts in storage.

benchmarks.name: Raft Consensus
benchmarks.value: Full implementation — 5 core modules + 8 advanced modules. 106 replication tests across 13 files
benchmarks.source: PR #218 (core) + PR #239 (entry dedup, depth-weighted election) + PR #247 (topology governor, state publisher). Core: raft.rs (432 LOC, 8 tests), node.rs (647 LOC, 10 tests), snapshot.rs (215 LOC, 4 tests), persistence.rs (214 LOC, 6 tests), rpc.rs (340 LOC). Advanced: chain_group.rs (536 LOC, 10 tests), commit_certificate.rs (659 LOC, 13 tests), entry_dedup.rs (732 LOC, 15 tests — WN-RAFT2-02), expected_composition.rs (569 LOC, 13 tests), typed_entry.rs (458 LOC, 12 tests), topology_governor.rs (357 LOC, 8 tests — WN-RAFT2-04), state_publisher.rs (330 LOC, 4 tests — WN-RAFT2-07), engine.rs (451 LOC, 3 tests). Total: 4,896 LOC, 106 tests.

benchmarks.name: Self-Governing Topology (WN-RAFT2-04)
benchmarks.value: TopologyGovernor: auto-demotion/promotion based on NodeHealth. 8 tests
benchmarks.source: PR #247. Axiom 4 (Chains as Things): the topology IS a governable entity. NodeHealth tracks: heartbeat freshness, replication lag, conflict rate, power state. Auto-demotes stale/conflicting/low-power nodes to Learner (zero consensus CPU). Auto-promotes after stability window (promotion_stability_count, default 10 consecutive healthy evaluations — hysteresis prevents oscillation). min_voters floor prevents quorum loss. Demotion triggers: heartbeat >5s, lag >100 entries, conflict >30%, Low Power Mode.

benchmarks.name: State-as-Intelligence Publisher (WN-RAFT2-07)
benchmarks.value: Publishes ElectionEvent, ReplicationSnapshot, MeshHealth as queryable graph entities. 4 tests
benchmarks.source: PR #247. Axiom 7 (Implicit-to-Explicit): makes Raft operational telemetry queryable. Publishes to PREFIX_RAFT_STATE_PUB (0x0E). ElectionEvent: term, candidate, votes, won, duration. ReplicationSnapshot: per-follower lag, throughput, commit_index. MeshHealth: aggregate confidence (0.0-1.0), voter/learner/suspect counts, total_lag, avg_conflict_rate. Enables 'show me all elections in last week' graph queries and mesh health dashboards.

comparisons.source: CLI tool, not a data entity

benchmarks.name: Entry Deduplication (WN-RAFT2-02)
benchmarks.value: 3 strategies: LWW, highest-sequence, highest-confidence. Collapses duplicate keys in commit batches. 15 tests
benchmarks.source: PR #239. entry_dedup.rs (732 LOC). Collapses duplicate keys in commit batches before store application — saves I/O, avoids unnecessary WAL entries. Three strategies: last-write-wins (LWW), highest-sequence-number, highest-confidence (epistemic-aware dedup unique to TrustDB). Handles cross-partition duplicates.

benchmarks.name: Device Lifecycle
benchmarks.value: Full implementation — register, deregister, heartbeat, stale detection. 10 device tests
benchmarks.source: PR #218. WN-DEVICE-01 to WN-DEVICE-03. register_device() with duplicate detection, deregister_device() with re-registration support, device_heartbeat() with configurable interval, stale_devices() detection. Device registry (device_registry.rs) manages entire lifecycle. Tests: register, duplicate rejection, get, deregister, re-register, heartbeat, stale detection, scope update, membership change.

benchmarks.name: Offline Mode (OCSS)
benchmarks.value: 4-phase sync protocol — 13 unit tests, 14 integration tests
benchmarks.source: PR #165 + PR #169. OCSS tags offline writes with 0.9x confidence penalty, restores on clean sync. 4 phases: delta computation, conflict classification, confidence restoration, gap manifest. All phases wired to store with real clearance extraction (PREFIX_CLEARANCE index), not hardcoded.

benchmarks.name: Federated Mesh (FWGA)
benchmarks.value: 5-tier DeviceManifest, CBAC clearance-enforced sync, quorum-as-confidence thresholds
benchmarks.source: PR #169 + PR #184. DeviceManifest with 5 tiers (Mothership/Regional/Desktop/Mobile/EdgeSensor), 6 unit tests. LoR-scoped data partitioning, clearance filtering, classification gates. Quorum replaced by continuous confidence threshold (Well: 0.67, Property: 0.67, Person: 0.0). Gap manifest filtered through manifest scope. Watermark lifecycle (persist + read back). Offline duration enforcement (writes >7d rejected).

comparisons.value: No clearance, no governance, LWW only
No device scoping, no confidence thresholds

benchmarks.name: Background Sync Worker
benchmarks.value: tokio::spawn async task — 3 tokio tests, reconnect signal + 30s periodic fallback
benchmarks.source: PR #165. SyncWorker spawned via tokio::spawn, SyncWorkerHandle for caller interaction (queue_write, notify_reconnect, shutdown). Failed sessions re-queue writes — no silent data loss. SyncReport emitted per session via mpsc channel.

benchmarks.name: Conflict Resolution Pipeline
benchmarks.value: Self-draining: 4-way classification + tolerance-band auto-merge + confidence-wins + quorum promotion. PREFIX_CONFLICT_QUEUE + PREFIX_SNAPSHOT + PREFIX_PROVISIONAL
benchmarks.source: PR #169 + PR #201. PeerConflicts stored in PREFIX_CONFLICT_QUEUE (0x06). AuthoritativelySuperseded in PREFIX_SNAPSHOT (0x07). NEW in PR #201: (1) WN-MESH-10 tolerance-band auto-merge — numeric PeerConflicts within 5% band → confidence-weighted average (MergedToleranceBand). (2) WN-MESH-11 confidence-wins — delta > 0.2 → higher side commits, lower snapshotted (MergedConfidenceWins). (3) WN-MESH-14 quorum promotion — PREFIX_PROVISIONAL (0x08) tracks provisional keys, post-sync scan auto-promotes when confidence crosses canonical threshold. (4) WN-MESH-13 stigmergic gap manifest — sorted by RRMS access heat for mobile reconnect priority. 26 sync_ops unit tests (6 new).

comparisons.source: Developer writes handlers, no built-in governance

benchmarks.name: Automatic Rebalancing
benchmarks.value: Scope-change detection + auto-rebalance trigger + Raft membership changes. 10 device tests cover all paths
benchmarks.source: PR #218. WN-REBAL-01: update_device_scope() detects ScopeChangeDelta (added/removed LoR subtrees). WN-REBAL-02: sync_with_manifest auto-triggered on scope change, gap manifest filtered through updated manifest. WN-REBAL-03: build_membership_change() produces ConfigChange entries for Raft joint consensus. Tests: scope update, membership change proposal.

sections.narrative: Availability has transformed from the original biggest gap to a flagship capability with 106 replication tests across 13 modules. The Raft implementation is now comprehensive: core consensus (leader election, log replication, compaction, persistence), plus advanced features — self-governing topology (WN-RAFT2-04: auto-demotion/promotion based on heartbeat, lag, conflict rate, and power state), state-as-intelligence publisher (WN-RAFT2-07: Raft telemetry as queryable graph entities), entry deduplication (WN-RAFT2-02: 3 strategies including highest-confidence), chain groups, commit certificates, expected composition tracking, and typed entry encoding. Device lifecycle (register/deregister/heartbeat/stale — 22 device tests), automatic rebalancing (scope-change detection + Raft membership changes), and a self-draining conflict pipeline (tolerance-band auto-merge, confidence-wins, quorum promotion — PR #201). The federated mesh (FWGA) provides 5-tier device hierarchy with CBAC-enforced sync, quorum-as-confidence thresholds, and stigmergic gap manifests. Unlike CouchDB/PouchDB (document sync, no governance), Cassandra (eventual consistency), or etcd (no self-governing topology), TrustDB's availability model enforces clearance at the storage layer while the topology governs itself. Remaining work: production hardening under real network partitions.

benchmarks.name: Auto WAL Recovery
benchmarks.value: Active

benchmarks.name: Data Checksums + WAL HMAC
benchmarks.value: CRC32c (pages) + HMAC-SHA384 chained WAL (PR #247). 33 WAL tests total
benchmarks.source: PR #247. Pages: CRC32c checksums + epoch superblock CRC32c. WAL: HMAC-SHA384 chained format — each record's tag covers prev_tag + WalProvenance + data, making any tampering cryptographically detectable. CNSA 2.0 compliant.

benchmarks.name: Resilience Test Suite
benchmarks.value: 13/13 DB-Gym pass

benchmarks.name: Transitive Invalidation Cascade
benchmarks.value: Automatic (stale_indices)

sections.narrative: Fault tolerance is a core strength. Auto WAL recovery and CRC32c checksums match RocksDB. TrustDB goes further with automatic transitive invalidation cascades — when upstream data changes, downstream dependent data is automatically marked stale. In every competitor, this is an application-layer responsibility. The DB-Gym resilience suite is custom-built for TrustDB's specific failure modes (confidence corruption, provenance chain breaks, cascade loops).
